s1_b1: Two balls collide. We already know one rule never breaks: total momentum stays the same.
s1_b2: But momentum doesn't tell the whole story of the crash. Listen to the difference.
s1_b3: Two steel balls click and bounce apart, lively as before.
s1_b4: Two balls of clay hit and just — stop. Stuck together. Dead.
s1_b5: Momentum was conserved in both. So the difference isn't there. It's in the energy of motion.
s1_b6: The steel balls kept almost all their motion energy. We call that an elastic collision.
s1_b7: The clay lost most of it — to heat, to sound, to bending. An inelastic collision.
s1_b8: And the extreme: when they stick and move off as one. Perfectly inelastic. The most energy lost.
s1_b9: The energy was never destroyed. It left the motion and became heat, deformation, noise.
s1_b10: So a collision has two questions. Momentum: always conserved. Motion energy: only sometimes.
s1_b11: Real life lives in between. Most collisions keep some bounce and lose some energy.
s1_b12: Momentum always balances; energy of motion is the variable. Solving each type with the right pair of rules — that's yours.
